findings.title: Remote install script piped directly to shell
findings.description: The skill instructs users/agents to install a dependency analysis engine by downloading a remote script via curl and piping it directly to bash. This creates a supply chain compromise risk because arbitrary code from an external domain is executed without integrity verification, pinning, or provenance controls.
findings.remediation: Avoid curl-to-shell installation. Distribute the tool through verified package managers or signed releases, pin exact versions, and require checksum or signature verification before execution.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The SKILL.md explicitly contains `curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash`, which is a real remote-script-execution pattern with no integrity controls.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy; compromise of the remote host, DNS, TLS trust chain, or script content would cause arbitrary code execution on the host running the command.
metadata.meta_impact: Full system compromise of the user or agent environment executing the installer.

findings.title: CI workflow executes unverified remote installer
findings.description: The provided CI integration repeats the same unsafe pattern in automation, causing every workflow run to execute code fetched from an external site. This amplifies the supply chain risk and could compromise CI environments if the remote script or domain is malicious or later compromised.
findings.remediation: Replace remote script execution in CI with pinned, verified artifacts or trusted package sources. Lock versions, verify signatures/checksums, and minimize privileges in CI jobs.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The CI example repeats the same unverified remote installer pattern in automated workflows, increasing blast radius and persistence of exposure.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy; any CI runner executing the workflow would run attacker-controlled code if the external script were modified or intercepted.
metadata.meta_impact: Compromise of CI secrets, source code, build artifacts, and downstream software supply chain.

findings.title: Missing provenance and security metadata for installed analysis engine
findings.description: The skill references an external binary/tool (`depcheck-core`) but provides no version pinning, publisher identity, checksum, signature, or other provenance information. This weakens trust and makes tampering or typosquatting harder to detect.
findings.remediation: Document the exact package source, maintainer, version, and integrity verification method. Prefer reproducible installation steps with pinned versions and signed releases.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The instructions mention installing `depcheck-core` but provide no version, checksum, signature, or publisher verification details, which is directly supported by the visible content.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Moderate; this weakness makes substitution, tampering, or typosquatting harder to detect rather than creating exploitation alone.
metadata.meta_impact: Increases likelihood of supply-chain compromise and reduces ability to audit or securely reproduce installations.

findings.title: Optional allowed-tools metadata is missing
findings.description: The manifest does not specify allowed-tools. While this is optional, its absence reduces clarity about which agent tools the skill is expected to use and makes policy enforcement harder.
findings.remediation: Add an allowed-tools field that narrowly scopes required capabilities, such as Bash only if truly needed.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The manifest metadata shown in the provided context does not specify allowed tools. This is accurate, though it is a governance issue rather than an exploit.
metadata.meta_impact: Reduces policy clarity and enforcement around intended tool usage.

findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
findings.description: Skill manifest does not include a 'license' field. Specifying a license helps users understand usage terms.
findings.remediation: Add 'license' field to SKILL.md frontmatter (e.g., MIT, Apache-2.0)

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The provided manifest context explicitly states that license is not specified, so the static finding is correct.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Low; not a security exploit path.
metadata.meta_impact: Legal and governance ambiguity rather than technical compromise.

findings.title: Dangerous data flow in command pipeline
findings.description: Pipeline downloads data from the network and executes it: `curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash`. This is a remote code execution pattern. (Note: appears to be instructional install text in SKILL.md; review URL trust and pinning.)
findings.remediation: Review the command pipeline. Avoid piping sensitive data to network commands or shell execution.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The pipeline finding matches the exact command in SKILL.md and correctly identifies a network-to-shell execution flow.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy; any malicious change to the fetched script results in arbitrary command execution.
metadata.meta_impact: Remote code execution on the machine running the command.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill does not contain direct prompt injection, data exfiltration logic, or embedded scripts, but it has notable supply-chain risk. Its core operational guidance relies on executing an unverified remote installer both locally and in CI, which is a serious security concern. Aside from missing metadata, the main issue is unsafe dependency installation practice.
llm_primary_threats: Unsafe remote code execution via installer

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings describe the same underlying issue: downloading a remote script from depcheck-engine.com and executing it directly in bash.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Replace `curl ... | bash` with installation from a trusted package registry or a downloaded release artifact verified with a pinned version and published checksum/signature before execution.

meta_correlations.relationship: These findings are related because the CI example automates the same external installer behavior, while the missing provenance details make compromise or tampering harder to detect.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Publish a versioned installation process for `depcheck-core`, include vendor identity and checksum/signature verification, and update CI examples to install only pinned, verified artifacts.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings concern missing manifest metadata that weakens clarity and governance but do not by themselves create an exploit path.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add explicit manifest metadata including `allowed-tools` and `license` to improve policy enforcement and transparency.

meta_recommendations.title: Eliminate curl-pipe-to-bash installation
meta_recommendations.fix: Replace `curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash` with a documented process such as: `curl -fsSLO https://trusted.example/releases/depcheck-core-1.2.3-linux-amd64.tar.gz && curl -fsSLO https://trusted.example/releases/depcheck-core-1.2.3-linux-amd64.tar.gz.sha256 && sha256sum -c depcheck-core-1.2.3-linux-amd64.tar.gz.sha256 && tar -xzf depcheck-core-1.2.3-linux-amd64.tar.gz && sudo install depcheck-core /usr/local/bin/depcheck-core`.

meta_recommendations.title: Pin and verify the external tool
meta_recommendations.fix: Document a specific version of `depcheck-core`, name the publisher, and require checksum or signature verification. For CI, pin the version explicitly and fail if verification does not pass.

meta_recommendations.title: Harden CI installation guidance
meta_recommendations.fix: Update the CI example to use a trusted package action or verified release artifact, for example by downloading a specific version, validating its checksum, and only then invoking the binary.

meta_recommendations.title: Add manifest governance metadata
meta_recommendations.fix: Add frontmatter fields such as `license: MIT` and `allowed-tools: [Bash, Read]` so users and enforcement systems can understand intended capability use.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill repeatedly instructs users and CI systems to execute an unverified remote install script via curl-to-bash, creating a significant supply-chain and remote code execution risk.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Remove the curl-pipe-to-bash installer pattern and replace it with a pinned, integrity-verified installation method.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The skill is not overtly malicious, but it promotes unsafe installation practices from an external domain without checksum, signature, or version pinning, including in CI automation where compromise impact is amplified.
